answer:
Designing a Practical Refresh Token Implementation for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

A Refresh Token is a long-lived credential issued alongside a short-lived Access Token during authentication. When the Access Token expires (typically 15-30 minutes), the client uses the Refresh Token to obtain a new Access Token without forcing the user to re-enter credentials. This pattern balances security (short-lived access tokens limit damage if stolen) with usability (users stay logged in for days or weeks). For a mid-size product, the implementation must handle token rotation, secure storage, revocation, and concurrent refresh scenarios.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, employees and managers accessed the Angular 18 SPA throughout their workday — submitting leave requests, approving them, and checking balances. Initially, Manoj used a single JWT with a 24-hour expiry, but this was insecure: a stolen token could be used for an entire day. He redesigned the auth flow with a dual-token strategy:

  • Access Token: 15-minute expiry, stored in memory (Angular service variable), sent via Authorization: Bearer header.
  • Refresh Token: 7-day expiry, stored in an httpOnly, secure, sameSite=strict cookie, sent automatically on POST /api/auth/refresh.

The Fastify backend stored refresh tokens in PostgreSQL via Prisma (hashed with bcrypt), enabling server-side revocation when an employee left the company or when HR needed to force-logout all sessions. Socket.io connections also validated the access token on every reconnect, ensuring real-time leave notifications respected token expiry.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// auth.ts — Fastify Refresh Token implementation
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import jwt from '@fastify/jwt';
import cookie from '@fastify/cookie';
import bcrypt from 'bcrypt';
import crypto from 'crypto';
import { prisma } from './db';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
app.register(jwt, { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET! });
app.register(cookie, { secret: process.env.COOKIE_SECRET! });

const ACCESS_TOKEN_TTL = '15m';
const REFRESH_TOKEN_DAYS = 7;

// Login — issue both tokens
app.post('/api/auth/login', async (req, reply) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body as any;
  const user = await prisma.employee.findUnique({ where: { email } });
  if (!user || !(await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash))) {
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  }

  // Generate access token (short-lived, in response body)
  const accessToken = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: user.id, role: user.role, department: user.department },
    { expiresIn: ACCESS_TOKEN_TTL }
  );

  // Generate refresh token (long-lived, in httpOnly cookie)
  const refreshToken = crypto.randomBytes(64).toString('hex');
  const refreshHash = await bcrypt.hash(refreshToken, 10);

  await prisma.refreshToken.create({
    data: {
      tokenHash: refreshHash,
      userId: user.id,
      expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + REFRESH_TOKEN_DAYS * 86400000),
      family: crypto.randomUUID(), // for rotation detection
    },
  });

  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', refreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true,
    secure: true,
    sameSite: 'strict',
    path: '/api/auth/refresh',
    maxAge: REFRESH_TOKEN_DAYS * 86400,
  });

  return { accessToken, expiresIn: 900 };
});

// Refresh — rotate tokens
app.post('/api/auth/refresh', async (req, reply) => {
  const oldToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (!oldToken) return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'No refresh token' });

  // Find matching token in DB
  const storedTokens = await prisma.refreshToken.findMany({
    where: { userId: { not: undefined }, revoked: false },
    orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' },
  });

  let matched = null;
  for (const stored of storedTokens) {
    if (await bcrypt.compare(oldToken, stored.tokenHash)) {
      matched = stored;
      break;
    }
  }

  if (!matched || matched.expiresAt < new Date()) {
    // Possible token reuse — revoke entire family
    if (matched) {
      await prisma.refreshToken.updateMany({
        where: { family: matched.family },
        data: { revoked: true },
      });
      req.log.warn({ family: matched.family }, 'Token reuse detected — family revoked');
    }
    reply.clearCookie('refreshToken', { path: '/api/auth/refresh' });
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid refresh token' });
  }

  // Revoke old token (rotation)
  await prisma.refreshToken.update({
    where: { id: matched.id },
    data: { revoked: true },
  });

  // Issue new pair
  const user = await prisma.employee.findUnique({ where: { id: matched.userId } });
  const accessToken = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: user!.id, role: user!.role, department: user!.department },
    { expiresIn: ACCESS_TOKEN_TTL }
  );

  const newRefreshToken = crypto.randomBytes(64).toString('hex');
  await prisma.refreshToken.create({
    data: {
      tokenHash: await bcrypt.hash(newRefreshToken, 10),
      userId: user!.id,
      expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + REFRESH_TOKEN_DAYS * 86400000),
      family: matched.family, // same family for rotation tracking
    },
  });

  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict',
    path: '/api/auth/refresh', maxAge: REFRESH_TOKEN_DAYS * 86400,
  });

  return { accessToken, expiresIn: 900 };
});

// Logout — revoke refresh token
app.post('/api/auth/logout', async (req, reply) => {
  const token = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (token) {
    const stored = await prisma.refreshToken.findMany({ where: { revoked: false } });
    for (const s of stored) {
      if (await bcrypt.compare(token, s.tokenHash)) {
        await prisma.refreshToken.updateMany({
          where: { family: s.family },
          data: { revoked: true },
        });
        break;
      }
    }
  }
  reply.clearCookie('refreshToken', { path: '/api/auth/refresh' });
  return { message: 'Logged out' };
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

During testing of the HR Leave System, Manoj discovered that users were being force-logged-out every 15 minutes despite having valid refresh tokens. The Angular interceptor was supposed to catch 401 responses and call /api/auth/refresh automatically, but it wasn't working.

  1. Symptom: After 15 minutes, every API call returned 401, and the user was redirected to login.
  2. Network tab analysis: The interceptor DID fire POST /api/auth/refresh, but the request had no cookie attached.
  3. Root cause: The refresh cookie was set with path: '/api/auth/refresh', but the Angular HttpClient call used a relative URL /auth/refresh (missing the /api prefix due to a proxy config). The browser didn't send the cookie because the path didn't match.
  4. Fix: Corrected the Angular environment's apiUrl to include /api and updated the interceptor to use the full path. Added an integration test that verified the cookie was present on refresh requests.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Refresh token stored in localStorage (accessible to XSS)Always use httpOnly + secure + sameSite=strict cookies; never expose refresh tokens to JavaScript
Concurrent requests trigger multiple refresh calls, causing token reuse detectionQueue refresh requests in Angular interceptor — first 401 triggers refresh, subsequent 401s wait for the refresh promise to resolve
Refresh token family revocation on legitimate multi-tab usageUse a BroadcastChannel in Angular to synchronize token refresh across tabs — only one tab refreshes, others listen for the new access token
Refresh tokens accumulate in database without cleanupRun a daily Prisma cron job: deleteMany({ where: { OR: [{ expiresAt: { lt: new Date() } }, { revoked: true, updatedAt: { lt: thirtyDaysAgo } }] } })

7. Validation Strategy

  • Token rotation test: Login → wait 15m → call API → verify interceptor refreshes → verify old refresh token is revoked in DB and new one is issued
  • Reuse detection test: Capture a refresh token, use it once (valid rotation), then replay the old token → verify all tokens in that family are revoked and user is force-logged-out
  • Cookie security test: Use Playwright to verify refresh token cookie has httpOnly, secure, and sameSite flags set correctly
  • Concurrent refresh test: Fire 5 simultaneous API calls after access token expires → verify only 1 refresh request is sent and all 5 calls succeed with the new token
  • Logout test: Logout → attempt to use the refresh cookie → verify 401 and cookie cleared

Debugging a Real Refresh Token Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Debugging Refresh Token issues requires understanding the multi-layer interaction between the client's token storage, the HTTP cookie mechanism, the server's token validation logic, and the database state. Common failure modes include cookie misconfiguration, race conditions during concurrent refreshes, clock skew between services, and token rotation detection misfires. In an interview, demonstrating structured debugging across these layers shows deep understanding of authentication architecture.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, a critical production issue emerged: managers on Safari were being logged out every 2 minutes while Chrome users worked fine. The Angular SPA used httpOnly cookies for refresh tokens and in-memory storage for access tokens. Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP) was silently blocking the refresh token cookie because the API domain was treated as a "third-party tracker" — the Fastify backend ran on api.hrleave.render.com while the Angular SPA was on hrleave.render.com.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// Step 1: Diagnostic middleware to log cookie presence
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req) => {
  req.log.info({
    path: req.url,
    hasCookie: !!req.cookies.refreshToken,
    userAgent: req.headers['user-agent']?.slice(0, 50),
    origin: req.headers.origin,
  }, 'Incoming request cookie diagnostic');
});

// Step 2: Identified the problem — cross-origin cookie blocked by Safari ITP
// BEFORE: SPA on hrleave.render.com, API on api-hrleave.render.com
// Safari ITP treats api-hrleave.render.com as third-party tracker

// AFTER FIX: Reverse proxy through the SPA's Render service
// render.yaml — add route rule
// routes:
//   - type: rewrite
//     source: /api/*
//     destination: https://api-hrleave.render.com/api/*

// Alternative fix for local dev — Fastify CORS + cookie config
app.register(require('@fastify/cors'), {
  origin: process.env.SPA_ORIGIN,  // exact origin, not wildcard
  credentials: true,               // allow cookies
});

// Updated cookie settings after fix
reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newToken, {
  httpOnly: true,
  secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production',
  sameSite: 'lax',   // changed from 'strict' to 'lax' for compatibility
  path: '/api/auth/refresh',
  maxAge: 7 * 86400,
  domain: '.hrleave.render.com',  // shared parent domain
});

// Step 3: Angular interceptor — queue concurrent refreshes
@Injectable()
export class AuthInterceptor implements HttpInterceptor {
  private isRefreshing = false;
  private refreshSubject$ = new BehaviorSubject<string | null>(null);

  intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler): Observable<HttpEvent<any>> {
    return next.handle(this.addToken(req)).pipe(
      catchError((err: HttpErrorResponse) => {
        if (err.status === 401 && !req.url.includes('/auth/refresh')) {
          return this.handleRefresh(req, next);
        }
        return throwError(() => err);
      })
    );
  }

  private handleRefresh(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
    if (!this.isRefreshing) {
      this.isRefreshing = true;
      this.refreshSubject$.next(null);

      return this.authService.refreshToken().pipe(
        switchMap((res) => {
          this.isRefreshing = false;
          this.refreshSubject$.next(res.accessToken);
          return next.handle(this.addToken(req));
        }),
        catchError(() => {
          this.isRefreshing = false;
          this.authService.logout();
          return throwError(() => new Error('Session expired'));
        })
      );
    }
    // Queue behind active refresh
    return this.refreshSubject$.pipe(
      filter(token => token !== null),
      take(1),
      switchMap(() => next.handle(this.addToken(req)))
    );
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj described his debugging process in a structured timeline:

  1. Ticket received: Manager on MacBook reports "app keeps asking me to login." Other managers on Windows laptops are fine.
  2. Environment correlation: Noticed all affected users were on Safari or iOS. Chrome on the same MacBook worked fine. Suspected browser-specific cookie behavior.
  3. Safari Web Inspector: Opened Storage tab → Cookies section showed the refresh token cookie was being set on login but silently removed after first navigation. Safari console showed a "cookie was blocked because it was set in a cross-site context" warning.
  4. Research: Safari ITP 2.3+ caps third-party cookies to 24 hours and may block them entirely for domains classified as trackers. Since the API ran on a different subdomain (api-hrleave vs hrleave), Safari treated it as cross-site.
  5. Fix deployed: Added a rewrite rule on Render to proxy /api/* requests through the SPA's origin. Now the cookie was set by the same origin, bypassing ITP entirely. All browsers worked identically.
  6. Regression test: Added a Playwright test running in WebKit (Safari engine) that logged in, waited 16 minutes (simulated with clock manipulation), and verified automatic token refresh succeeded.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Safari ITP silently blocks cross-subdomain cookiesServe API and SPA from the same origin using a reverse proxy; verify cookie behavior in Safari during QA
Access token stored in memory is lost on page refreshOn app bootstrap, call /api/auth/refresh to obtain a new access token — the refresh cookie persists across refreshes
Clock skew causes JWT to appear expired before actual TTLAdd a 30-second clock tolerance in JWT verification: app.jwt.verify(token, { clockTolerance: 30 })
Refresh endpoint has no rate limiting — vulnerable to brute forceApply @fastify/rate-limit on /api/auth/refresh: max 10 requests per minute per IP

7. Validation Strategy

  • Cross-browser test: Playwright tests running on Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit verifying full login → token expiry → silent refresh → continued API access
  • Cookie attribute audit: Automated test that inspects Set-Cookie response header on login and asserts HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, and correct Path
  • Safari-specific regression: Monthly manual check on real Safari (not just WebKit) to catch new ITP behavior changes
  • Monitoring: Track refresh endpoint success/failure ratio by User-Agent family — alert if any browser's failure rate exceeds 5%

Debugging a Real Socket.io Rooms Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Socket.io Rooms are server-side groupings of connected sockets. A socket can join multiple rooms, and the server can emit events to all sockets in a specific room using io.to(roomName).emit(event, data). Rooms are ephemeral — they exist only while at least one socket is joined and disappear when the last socket leaves. Debugging room issues requires understanding the connection lifecycle, room membership tracking, and the difference between socket.join(), io.to(), and socket.to() (which excludes the sender).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, Socket.io rooms were critical for real-time leave status notifications. When an employee submitted a leave request, their manager needed an instant notification. Manoj organized rooms by department: room:dept-engineering, room:dept-hr, etc. When a leave was approved or rejected, the server emitted to the employee's personal room room:user-{id} for the status update. The bug: managers in the HR department were not receiving notifications for cross-department leave requests they were responsible for approving.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — Broken: managers only join their own department room
io.on('connection', async (socket) => {
  const token = socket.handshake.auth.token;
  const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET!);
  const userId = decoded.sub;
  const role = decoded.role;
  const department = decoded.department;

  // Join personal room
  socket.join(`room:user-${userId}`);
  // BUG: Only joins own department — HR manager misses engineering leaves
  socket.join(`room:dept-${department}`);

  console.log(`${userId} joined rooms:`, Array.from(socket.rooms));
});

// Leave notification — emits to department room
async function notifyLeaveSubmitted(leave: any) {
  // This only reaches sockets in the employee's dept room
  io.to(`room:dept-${leave.department}`).emit('leave:submitted', {
    id: leave.id,
    employee: leave.employeeName,
    type: leave.type,
    dates: leave.dates,
  });
}

// AFTER — Fixed: managers join all managed department rooms + approver room
io.on('connection', async (socket) => {
  const token = socket.handshake.auth.token;
  const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET!);
  const userId = decoded.sub;
  const role = decoded.role;

  // Personal room (for direct notifications like approval status)
  socket.join(`room:user-${userId}`);

  if (role === 'manager' || role === 'hr') {
    // Fetch managed departments from DB
    const manager = await prisma.employee.findUnique({
      where: { id: userId },
      select: { managedDepartments: true },
    });

    // Join ALL managed department rooms
    for (const dept of manager?.managedDepartments ?? []) {
      socket.join(`room:dept-${dept}`);
    }

    // Also join a dedicated approver room
    socket.join(`room:approver-${userId}`);

    console.log(`Manager ${userId} joined rooms:`, Array.from(socket.rooms));
  } else {
    // Regular employees join only their department
    const emp = await prisma.employee.findUnique({
      where: { id: userId },
      select: { department: true },
    });
    socket.join(`room:dept-${emp?.department}`);
  }
});

// Improved notification — also targets the specific approver
async function notifyLeaveSubmitted(leave: any) {
  // Notify department room (colleagues see the leave)
  io.to(`room:dept-${leave.department}`).emit('leave:submitted', {
    id: leave.id, employee: leave.employeeName,
    type: leave.type, dates: leave.dates,
  });

  // Also notify the specific approver directly
  if (leave.approverId) {
    io.to(`room:approver-${leave.approverId}`).emit('leave:pending-approval', {
      id: leave.id, employee: leave.employeeName,
      type: leave.type, dates: leave.dates, urgent: leave.isUrgent,
    });
  }
}

// Debug utility — list all rooms and their member count
app.get('/debug/rooms', async (req, reply) => {
  const rooms = io.sockets.adapter.rooms;
  const roomList: Record<string, number> = {};
  rooms.forEach((sockets, roomName) => {
    if (!roomName.startsWith('room:')) return; // skip socket ID rooms
    roomList[roomName] = sockets.size;
  });
  return roomList;
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj walked through this debugging scenario step by step:

  1. Report: The HR manager complained: "I get notifications for HR team leaves but not for engineering or sales team leaves, even though I'm the approver for all departments."
  2. Quick check: Opened the Angular SPA on the HR manager's machine and the engineering employee's machine side-by-side. Engineering employee submitted a leave — HR manager's notification bell didn't update, but the engineering team lead's did.
  3. Server-side inspection: Hit the /debug/rooms endpoint — the HR manager's socket was only in room:user-mgr1 and room:dept-hr. Not in room:dept-engineering.
  4. Code review: The connection handler used decoded.department (a single string from the JWT) to determine room membership. The JWT only contained the manager's own department, not the departments they managed.
  5. Fix: Queried managedDepartments from the database on connection and joined all relevant rooms. Also added a dedicated room:approver-{id} room as a fallback so approvers always get notified regardless of room membership.
  6. Verification: Re-checked /debug/rooms — HR manager now in 5 rooms. Tested cross-department leave submission — notification appeared instantly.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Room membership derived from JWT claims which may be stale or incompleteAlways fetch current role/department data from the database on socket connection; don't rely solely on JWT claims for room assignment
Rooms disappear when the last socket disconnects — no persistent room stateRooms are meant to be ephemeral; rebuild membership on every connection. For critical notifications, also persist to DB and deliver on next login
Broadcasting to large rooms (100+ sockets) causes memory spikesUse Socket.io's Redis adapter (@socket.io/redis-adapter) to distribute room membership across multiple server instances
No visibility into room membership during production debuggingExpose a protected /debug/rooms endpoint and log room join/leave events with structured logging (Pino)

7. Validation Strategy

  • Room membership test: Connect as a manager with 3 managed departments → assert socket is in all 3 room:dept-* rooms plus personal and approver rooms
  • Cross-department notification test: Connect HR manager + engineering employee → employee submits leave → assert HR manager receives leave:submitted event within 1 second
  • Disconnect/reconnect test: Disconnect manager socket, reconnect → verify all room memberships are restored (not just the personal room)
  • Load test: Connect 50 sockets across 5 departments, submit 10 concurrent leaves → verify all appropriate sockets receive notifications with no duplicates
  • Playwright E2E: Login as HR manager, open dashboard, then in a separate context login as engineering employee and submit leave → verify notification badge updates on HR manager's screen

Comparing Two Socket.io Rooms Approaches with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Socket.io Rooms can be organized following two primary architectural approaches: Entity-Based Rooms (rooms named after domain entities like departments, projects, or teams — e.g., room:dept-engineering) versus Event-Based Rooms (rooms named after the type of event consumers want to receive — e.g., room:leave-notifications, room:approval-updates). The choice affects scalability, flexibility, and how tightly coupled the room structure is to the business domain.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the team debated between two approaches for organizing Socket.io rooms:

Approach A — Entity-Based Rooms: Rooms mapped to organizational structure: room:dept-{deptName} for department-wide broadcasts, room:user-{userId} for personal notifications, and room:team-{managerId} for a manager's direct reports. When a leave was submitted, the server emitted to room:dept-engineering and room:user-{approverId}.

Approach B — Event-Based Rooms: Rooms mapped to notification types: room:leave-submissions (for anyone interested in new leaves), room:approval-updates (for employees awaiting approval), room:balance-changes (for HR tracking balance usage). Users subscribed to the events they cared about rather than being auto-assigned by department.

Manoj chose Approach A (Entity-Based) because the HR domain had clear organizational boundaries and the primary use case was department-scoped broadcasting.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// APPROACH A — Entity-Based Rooms (Chosen for HR Leave System)
// Room assignment happens on connection based on org structure

import { Server } from 'socket.io';
import { prisma } from './db';

const io = new Server(server, { cors: { origin: process.env.SPA_ORIGIN } });

io.on('connection', async (socket) => {
  const decoded = verifyToken(socket.handshake.auth.token);
  const user = await prisma.employee.findUnique({
    where: { id: decoded.sub },
    select: { id: true, role: true, department: true, managedDepartments: true },
  });

  if (!user) { socket.disconnect(); return; }

  // Personal room — always joined
  socket.join(`room:user-${user.id}`);

  // Department room — based on org membership
  socket.join(`room:dept-${user.department}`);

  // Managers join all departments they oversee
  if (user.role === 'manager' || user.role === 'hr') {
    for (const dept of user.managedDepartments) {
      socket.join(`room:dept-${dept}`);
    }
  }

  // HR admins join a global room for all leave events
  if (user.role === 'hr') {
    socket.join('room:hr-global');
  }

  socket.on('disconnect', () => {
    console.log(`User ${user.id} disconnected, left all rooms`);
  });
});

// Notification dispatch — targets entity rooms
function emitLeaveEvent(leave: any) {
  // Department-wide: colleagues see who is on leave
  io.to(`room:dept-${leave.department}`).emit('leave:new', {
    employee: leave.employeeName,
    dates: leave.dates,
    type: leave.type,
  });

  // Direct approver notification
  io.to(`room:user-${leave.approverId}`).emit('leave:pending', {
    id: leave.id,
    employee: leave.employeeName,
    action: 'approve_or_reject',
  });

  // HR global feed
  io.to('room:hr-global').emit('leave:logged', { id: leave.id });
}

// APPROACH B — Event-Based Rooms (alternative, not chosen)
// Shown for comparison — users subscribe to event channels
/*
io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  socket.on('subscribe', (channels: string[]) => {
    for (const ch of channels) {
      if (allowedChannels.includes(ch)) {
        socket.join(`event:${ch}`);
      }
    }
  });

  socket.on('unsubscribe', (channel: string) => {
    socket.leave(`event:${channel}`);
  });
});

// Notification dispatch — targets event channels
function emitLeaveEvent(leave: any) {
  io.to('event:leave-submissions').emit('leave:new', { ... });
  io.to('event:approval-updates').emit('leave:pending', { ... });
}
*/

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj initially considered the Event-Based approach because it seemed more flexible. During a prototype sprint, he implemented both and ran a 2-day comparison:

  1. Event-Based issue: New employees didn't know which channels to subscribe to. The Angular SPA had to maintain a mapping of "role → default subscriptions" which duplicated the server-side org logic. When HR added a new notification type (leave:escalated), existing users didn't receive it until they manually subscribed.
  2. Entity-Based advantage: Room assignment was automatic on connection — no client-side subscription logic needed. When a new department was created, managers were automatically added to the room on their next login because the room assignment queried the database.
  3. Hybrid consideration: Manoj briefly considered a hybrid where entity rooms handled mandatory notifications and event rooms handled optional preferences (like "notify me of all company-wide leaves"). He deferred this to a future iteration to keep the initial implementation simple.
  4. Decision rationale: The HR domain had a clear hierarchy (company → department → team → individual) that mapped perfectly to entity rooms. Event-based rooms would be better for a product like a chat app where users choose channels freely.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Entity-based rooms break when org structure changes (dept rename, merge)Use department IDs (not names) as room keys: room:dept-{id}. Renaming a department doesn't affect room membership
Managers with 10+ departments create many room joins on connectionBatch joins using socket.join([room1, room2, ...]) (Socket.io accepts arrays); limit to 20 rooms max per socket
Event-based rooms require client to know available channels upfrontIf using event-based: expose a GET /api/notification-channels endpoint; auto-subscribe on connection based on user's role
No way to audit who is in which room at any given timeLog room join/leave events to a structured log (Pino); expose /debug/rooms endpoint showing room → socket count mapping

7. Validation Strategy

  • Room assignment test: Connect as employee → verify 2 rooms (personal + dept). Connect as HR manager with 3 managed depts → verify 6 rooms (personal + own dept + 3 managed + hr-global)
  • Notification delivery test: Submit leave in engineering dept → verify engineering room sockets receive event, HR room does NOT unless manager manages engineering
  • Org change test: Add a new department to a manager's managedDepartments, disconnect and reconnect → verify new room is joined automatically
  • Performance test: Connect 100 sockets across 10 departments, emit to one department room → verify only sockets in that room receive the event (no leakage) and delivery completes in under 100ms
  • Playwright E2E: Open two browser tabs — manager and employee. Employee submits leave. Verify manager tab shows notification badge within 2 seconds.

Designing a Practical API Versioning Implementation for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

API Versioning is the practice of managing changes to an API's contract so that existing consumers continue to work while new features are introduced. The three common strategies are: URL path versioning (/api/v1/resource), header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api.v2+json), and query parameter versioning (/api/resource?version=2). For a mid-size product with a small number of consumers, URL path versioning offers the best balance of simplicity, discoverability, and tooling support.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the Express.js API initially served only the Angular 18 SPA. When the company decided to build a mobile app and allow third-party CRM integrations, the existing API needed breaking changes: the GET /api/leads response format needed restructuring (nested contact info instead of flat fields), pagination changed from offset-based to cursor-based, and several deprecated fields needed removal.

Manoj implemented URL path versioning with /api/v1/ (existing behavior for the Angular SPA) and /api/v2/ (new structure for mobile and integrations). The v1 routes were marked as deprecated with a sunset header, giving the Angular SPA team 3 months to migrate. Both versions shared the same MongoDB models and service layer — only the route handlers and response transformers differed.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// app.ts — Express.js API versioning setup
import express from 'express';
import v1Routes from './routes/v1';
import v2Routes from './routes/v2';

const app = express();

// Deprecation middleware for v1
app.use('/api/v1', (req, res, next) => {
  res.set('Deprecation', 'true');
  res.set('Sunset', 'Sat, 30 Jun 2026 00:00:00 GMT');
  res.set('Link', '</api/v2>; rel="successor-version"');
  next();
});

app.use('/api/v1', v1Routes);
app.use('/api/v2', v2Routes);

// Redirect unversioned requests to latest version
app.use('/api/leads', (req, res) => {
  res.redirect(308, `/api/v2/leads${req.url === '/' ? '' : req.url}`);
});

// routes/v1/leads.ts — V1 handler (flat response, offset pagination)
import { Router } from 'express';
import { LeadService } from '../../services/LeadService';

const router = Router();
const leadService = new LeadService();

router.get('/leads', async (req, res) => {
  const { page = '1', limit = '20' } = req.query as any;
  const { leads, total } = await leadService.findAll({
    page: +page, limit: +limit,
  });

  // V1 response: flat structure
  res.json({
    data: leads.map(lead => ({
      id: lead._id,
      name: lead.name,
      email: lead.contactEmail,        // flat
      phone: lead.contactPhone,        // flat
      company: lead.companyName,       // flat
      stage: lead.stage,
      value: lead.estimatedValue,
    })),
    page: +page,
    totalPages: Math.ceil(total / +limit),
    total,
  });
});

export default router;

// routes/v2/leads.ts — V2 handler (nested response, cursor pagination)
import { Router } from 'express';
import { LeadService } from '../../services/LeadService';

const router = Router();
const leadService = new LeadService();

router.get('/leads', async (req, res) => {
  const { cursor, limit = '20' } = req.query as any;
  const { leads, nextCursor, hasMore } = await leadService.findWithCursor({
    cursor, limit: +limit,
  });

  // V2 response: nested structure
  res.json({
    data: leads.map(lead => ({
      id: lead._id,
      name: lead.name,
      contact: {                        // nested object
        email: lead.contactEmail,
        phone: lead.contactPhone,
      },
      company: {
        name: lead.companyName,
        industry: lead.companyIndustry,  // new field in v2
      },
      pipeline: {
        stage: lead.stage,
        value: lead.estimatedValue,
        probability: lead.closeProbability,  // new in v2
      },
    })),
    pagination: {
      nextCursor,
      hasMore,
      limit: +limit,
    },
  });
});

export default router;

// services/LeadService.ts — Shared, version-agnostic
export class LeadService {
  async findAll({ page, limit }: { page: number; limit: number }) {
    const skip = (page - 1) * limit;
    const [leads, total] = await Promise.all([
      Lead.find().sort({ createdAt: -1 }).skip(skip).limit(limit).lean(),
      Lead.countDocuments(),
    ]);
    return { leads, total };
  }

  async findWithCursor({ cursor, limit }: { cursor?: string; limit: number }) {
    const query = cursor ? { _id: { $lt: cursor } } : {};
    const leads = await Lead.find(query)
      .sort({ _id: -1 }).limit(limit + 1).lean();
    const hasMore = leads.length > limit;
    if (hasMore) leads.pop();
    const nextCursor = hasMore ? leads[leads.length - 1]._id : null;
    return { leads, nextCursor, hasMore };
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After deploying v2 alongside v1, Manoj encountered an issue: the Angular SPA's Recharts pipeline visualization broke because a shared Angular service was inadvertently calling /api/v2/leads instead of /api/v1/leads. The chart expected flat fields (lead.email) but received nested objects (lead.contact.email), causing undefined labels in the chart.

  1. Detection: QA noticed the pipeline chart showed "undefined" for lead contact info after the v2 deployment.
  2. Diagnosis: Browser Network tab showed the Recharts component was calling /api/v2/leads. Traced the Angular service — someone had changed the base URL in environment.ts from /api/v1 to /api/v2 globally, affecting all services.
  3. Fix: Reverted the global base URL change. Instead, created separate environment variables: API_V1_URL for legacy Angular services and API_V2_URL for new mobile-facing services. Added a lint rule to prevent hardcoding API version in service files.
  4. Prevention: Added response-shape validation in Angular services using Zod schemas so mismatches would throw a descriptive error instead of silent undefined values.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Maintaining two versions doubles the bug-fix surface areaShare the service layer between v1 and v2; only the route handlers and response transformers are version-specific. Bug fixes in the service layer apply to both versions
Clients don't know when a version is deprecatedSet Deprecation, Sunset, and Link headers on deprecated versions; log deprecation warnings server-side to track which clients still use old versions
Unversioned endpoints create ambiguityRedirect /api/leads to /api/v2/leads with 308 Permanent Redirect; never expose endpoints without a version prefix
Global base URL change accidentally migrates all clients to v2Use per-service version configuration in Angular: LeadService.API_VERSION = 'v1'. Enforce via code review and lint rules

7. Validation Strategy

  • Contract test: Define JSON schemas for v1 and v2 responses; run schema validation tests against both endpoints to detect accidental response shape changes
  • Deprecation header test: Assert all /api/v1/* responses include Deprecation: true and Sunset headers
  • Redirect test: Verify GET /api/leads returns 308 redirect to /api/v2/leads
  • Backward compatibility test: Run the full Angular E2E test suite against /api/v1 to ensure existing behavior is preserved after v2 deployment
  • Client migration tracking: Log x-client-version header from Angular and mobile apps; dashboard showing percentage of traffic still on v1 to track migration progress

Debugging a Real API Versioning Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Debugging API versioning issues requires understanding routing precedence, response contract mismatches, and client-version confusion. Common failure modes include: route middleware applying to the wrong version, shared services leaking version-specific behavior, clients accidentally calling the wrong version endpoint, and missing backward-compatible fallbacks. In an interview, walking through a real versioning incident demonstrates operational maturity and API design discipline.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, after deploying API v2 with cursor-based pagination, the third-party CRM integration partner reported that their webhook receiver was rejecting deal-update callbacks. The partner's system expected the v1 payload format (flat fields), but Manoj's webhook dispatcher was using the v2 response transformer for ALL outgoing webhooks, regardless of which API version the partner had registered with.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — Broken: webhook dispatcher uses v2 transformer globally
import { transformLeadV2 } from '../transformers/v2';

async function dispatchWebhook(event: string, lead: any) {
  const subscribers = await WebhookSubscription.find({ event });

  for (const sub of subscribers) {
    // BUG: Always sends v2 format — v1 subscribers get unexpected fields
    const payload = transformLeadV2(lead);

    await fetch(sub.callbackUrl, {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({ event, data: payload }),
    });
  }
}

// AFTER — Fixed: version-aware webhook dispatcher
import { transformLeadV1 } from '../transformers/v1';
import { transformLeadV2 } from '../transformers/v2';

const transformers: Record<string, (lead: any) => any> = {
  v1: transformLeadV1,
  v2: transformLeadV2,
};

async function dispatchWebhook(event: string, lead: any) {
  const subscribers = await WebhookSubscription.find({ event });

  for (const sub of subscribers) {
    // Use subscriber's registered API version
    const version = sub.apiVersion || 'v1'; // default to v1 for safety
    const transform = transformers[version] || transformers.v1;
    const payload = transform(lead);

    try {
      const response = await fetch(sub.callbackUrl, {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: {
          'Content-Type': 'application/json',
          'X-API-Version': version,
          'X-Webhook-Id': sub._id.toString(),
        },
        body: JSON.stringify({ event, data: payload, apiVersion: version }),
      });

      if (!response.ok) {
        console.error({
          webhookId: sub._id,
          callbackUrl: sub.callbackUrl,
          status: response.status,
          version,
        }, 'Webhook delivery failed');
      }
    } catch (err) {
      console.error({ webhookId: sub._id, err }, 'Webhook dispatch error');
    }
  }
}

// Migration script — add apiVersion to existing subscriptions
async function migrateWebhookSubscriptions() {
  const result = await WebhookSubscription.updateMany(
    { apiVersion: { $exists: false } },
    { $set: { apiVersion: 'v1' } } // existing subscribers default to v1
  );
  console.log(`Migrated ${result.modifiedCount} webhook subscriptions to v1`);
}

// transformers/v1.ts — Flat format
export function transformLeadV1(lead: any) {
  return {
    id: lead._id,
    name: lead.name,
    email: lead.contactEmail,
    phone: lead.contactPhone,
    company: lead.companyName,
    stage: lead.stage,
    value: lead.estimatedValue,
  };
}

// transformers/v2.ts — Nested format
export function transformLeadV2(lead: any) {
  return {
    id: lead._id,
    name: lead.name,
    contact: { email: lead.contactEmail, phone: lead.contactPhone },
    company: { name: lead.companyName, industry: lead.companyIndustry },
    pipeline: { stage: lead.stage, value: lead.estimatedValue,
      probability: lead.closeProbability },
  };
}

// Regression test
describe('Webhook dispatcher versioning', () => {
  it('sends v1 format to v1 subscribers', async () => {
    const captured: any[] = [];
    nock('https://partner.example.com')
      .post('/webhook', (body: any) => {
        captured.push(body);
        return true;
      }).reply(200);

    await WebhookSubscription.create({
      event: 'lead.updated',
      callbackUrl: 'https://partner.example.com/webhook',
      apiVersion: 'v1',
    });

    await dispatchWebhook('lead.updated', mockLead);

    expect(captured[0].data.email).toBeDefined();      // flat field
    expect(captured[0].data.contact).toBeUndefined();  // no nesting
    expect(captured[0].apiVersion).toBe('v1');
  });

  it('sends v2 format to v2 subscribers', async () => {
    const captured: any[] = [];
    nock('https://mobile.example.com')
      .post('/webhook', (body: any) => {
        captured.push(body);
        return true;
      }).reply(200);

    await WebhookSubscription.create({
      event: 'lead.updated',
      callbackUrl: 'https://mobile.example.com/webhook',
      apiVersion: 'v2',
    });

    await dispatchWebhook('lead.updated', mockLead);

    expect(captured[0].data.contact.email).toBeDefined(); // nested
    expect(captured[0].data.email).toBeUndefined();       // no flat field
    expect(captured[0].apiVersion).toBe('v2');
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj walked through this incident methodically:

  1. Alert received: The integration partner emailed: "Webhook payloads changed without notice. Our parser is failing on an unexpected contact object." They shared a sample payload showing the nested v2 format.
  2. Timeline correlation: The failure started exactly when Manoj deployed API v2. He checked the webhook dispatcher code and realized it imported only the v2 transformer — it had been refactored during the v2 work and the v1 transformer import was removed.
  3. Impact assessment: Only one partner was affected because they were the only active webhook subscriber. But the bug would have impacted any future v1 subscribers too.
  4. Root cause: The WebhookSubscription MongoDB schema had no apiVersion field. The dispatcher had no way to know which format each subscriber expected. When the developer switched the transformer to v2, it silently broke all existing subscribers.
  5. Fix and migration: Added apiVersion field to the schema, ran a migration to set all existing subscriptions to v1, and updated the dispatcher to select the transformer based on the subscriber's version. Also added the version to the webhook payload itself so subscribers could validate.
  6. Communication: Sent the partner an incident report with the fix timeline and a link to updated webhook documentation showing the apiVersion field in the subscription API.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Webhook payloads not version-aware — breaking change silently sent to all subscribersStore apiVersion per webhook subscription; dispatcher selects the correct response transformer based on the subscriber's version
Removing old transformer code during version upgradeNever delete v1 transformers until all subscribers have migrated; track migration status per subscriber in a dashboard
No integration tests covering webhook payload format per versionAdd parameterized tests that dispatch the same event and assert different payload shapes for v1 and v2 subscribers
Partners have no way to test webhooks before going liveExpose a POST /api/webhooks/test endpoint that sends a sample payload to the subscriber's callback URL using their registered version

7. Validation Strategy

  • Version isolation test: Register a v1 and v2 webhook subscriber for the same event; trigger the event; assert each receives their version-specific payload format
  • Migration test: Run the migration script on a test DB with 10 subscriptions missing apiVersion; verify all 10 get v1 assigned
  • Schema validation: Validate outgoing webhook payloads against JSON Schema definitions for each version before dispatching; log and skip malformed payloads
  • Partner sandbox: Provide a /api/webhooks/test endpoint so partners can trigger a test delivery and verify their parser handles the payload correctly
  • Monitoring: Track webhook delivery success rate per subscriber and version; alert if any subscriber's failure rate exceeds 10% (would have caught this issue within minutes)
